explanation: The property states that whenever (globally) the robot avoids a certain area ('areaAvoided'), it immediately provides an explanation ('explain'). This matches the requirement that if the robot avoids areas, it should explain why. Using G (globally), the implication for each occurrence of 'areaAvoided' is that 'explain' occurs at the same time (which is the most direct, in absence of timing information), i.e., G(areaAvoided -> explain).

The property 'G (areaAvoided -> F explain)' states that whenever the robot avoids a certain area (areaAvoided becomes true), it is required that at some point in the future, the robot provides an explanation (explain becomes true). This formulation captures the natural language requirement that if the robot avoids certain areas, it should explain why. The triggering condition is the avoidance action, and the response is providing an explanation. This reactive property ensures the correct temporal ordering and causality implied by the input natural language specification.

The property describes the requirement that whenever the robot avoids a certain area, it should also provide an explanation. In LTL, this is captured by stating that globally (at all times), if 'areaAvoided' occurs (the robot avoids an area), then 'explain' must occur (the robot provides an explanation). This matches the natural language which specifies a causality: avoidance should be accompanied by an explanation. The formula uses G (globally) to ensure the property holds at all times.

The natural language property says: 'If the robot avoids certain areas, it should explain why.' This is naturally captured in LTL by saying: whenever 'areaAvoided' happens (avoiding a certain area), then 'explain' (the robot providing an explanation) must eventually occur. In LTL, implication over time is usually captured as a response pattern: Globally, whenever areaAvoided holds, eventually explain will hold. That is, G (areaAvoided -> F explain). This ensures that every time the robot avoids an area, an explanation will follow at some point in the future. Previous or alternative translations might inappropriately require explain to hold at the same time or not guarantee the temporal relationship (e.g., using G(areaAvoided -> explain)), but 'eventually' is needed to allow the explanation to occur after avoidance, not necessarily at the same moment.

The input requires that whenever the robot avoids a certain area (i.e., 'areaAvoided' becomes true), it should provide an explanation ('explain'). The standard LTL way to encode this is using the implication with the 'globally' (G) operator: 
Globally, if 'areaAvoided' holds, then 'explain' must at least hold at the same time step (or possibly in the future if we read 'should explain why' as delayed). However, the most direct interpretation is that it must explain whenever it avoids an area, thus we use G(areaAvoided -> explain).

This formula ensures that at every moment, if the robot avoids a certain area, it must also provide an explanation at that same moment. This captures the natural language intent by guaranteeing the explanation requirement is always met when avoidance occurs. As the original LTL property, it doesn't allow for any time delay, so if temporal delay is allowed then the formula may be refined, but as written, it fits the prompt best.

The property expresses that whenever the robot avoids a certain area (areaAvoided), it should provide an explanation (explain). This is captured by saying 'globally, if areaAvoided happens, then eventually explain will occur.' This matches the intent of the natural language: every time the avoidance is observed, the explanation should (eventually) follow. Previous formulations might miss the necessity of the implication or eventuality aspect, but this directly enforces the relationship between avoidance and explanation.

The requirement states that whenever the robot avoids a certain area, it should provide an explanation. This is best captured by the formula G(areaAvoided -> F(explain)), which means 'Globally, if the robot avoids a certain area (areaAvoided), then eventually (at some point, possibly immediately) it must provide an explanation (explain).' This directly relates the two actions in temporal order, ensuring that every time the avoidance happens, an explanation follows at some point. Using implication with 'eventually' on the right-hand side is the standard way in LTL to require a response (explanation) to a trigger (area avoidance). No previous LTL was given to compare, but this formula precisely and minimally encodes the requirement.

The natural language statement says: 'If the robot avoids certain areas, it should explain why.' This specifies an implication: whenever 'areaAvoided' becomes true (the robot avoids an area), eventually 'explain' (the robot provides an explanation) should be true (since the robot should explain why it avoided the area, possibly after the fact, but not before). In LTL, this is best expressed using the 'G' (globally) operator for monitoring at all times and the implication 'areaAvoided -> F explain', which means at all times, if 'areaAvoided' is true, then eventually 'explain' will be true. This ensures that every avoidance is eventually accompanied by an explanation. No previous LTL properties have been suggested, so no further comparison is necessary.

The property specifies that whenever the robot avoids a certain area (areaAvoided), it should provide an explanation (explain). This is expressed by saying that it is always the case (G) that if areaAvoided holds, then explain must also hold, either at the same time or afterwards (eventually). The formula G (areaAvoided -> F explain) ensures that every instance of area avoidance by the robot leads to an explanation being provided. This captures the intent of the natural language, which requires an explanation whenever an area is avoided. This is a standard pattern in LTL for 'if A happens, then B should eventually happen.'

The property states that whenever the robot avoids a certain area (areaAvoided), it should eventually explain why (explain). This is captured by the formula G(areaAvoided -> F explain), which means globally, whenever areaAvoided becomes true, at some point in the future (possibly immediately), explain must also occur. This LTL property correctly reflects the requirement that an explanation is expected every time an area is avoided, without imposing restrictions before such avoidance events or attempting to use forbidden numerical comparisons. The formula also matches the instruction that explanations must be given if and only if an area is avoided, no more, no less.
